section.title: Accepting One Another
section.sectionRationale: Paul brings the discussion of the weak and the strong to its climax and its Christ-centered ground. The strong are not merely to endure the weak but to bear with their failings and to please their neighbor for his good, because Christ Himself did not please Himself. Then Paul lifts the whole matter into the sweep of redemption: Christ became a servant to the circumcised to confirm God's promises and so that the Gentiles might glorify God for His mercy. A chain of Old Testament quotations shows that Jew and Gentile praising God together was always the plan. The section ends in a benediction of joy, peace, and overflowing hope.
section.thesis: The strong ought to bear with the weak and please their neighbor for his upbuilding, following Christ who did not please Himself; and the whole church, Jew and Gentile, is to welcome one another as Christ welcomed them, so that with one voice they glorify the God of hope who fills them with joy and peace by the power of the Holy Spirit.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the strong bear with the weak because Christ did not please Himself; welcome one another as Christ welcomed you, confirmed by a chain of Scripture that Jew and Gentile would praise God together; and the benediction of the God of hope who fills His people with joy, peace, and overflowing hope.

units.label: Bear with the weak, as Christ did not please Himself
units.note: the Scriptures written for our hope

verses.text: We who are strong ought to {{bastazo|bear with}} the shortcomings of the weak and not to please ourselves.
Each of us should please his neighbor for his good, to {{oikodome|build him up}}.
For even Christ did not please Himself, but as it is written: “The insults of those who insult You have fallen on Me.”
For everything that was written in the past was written for our instruction, so that through endurance and the encouragement of the Scriptures, we might have hope.
Now may the God who gives endurance and encouragement grant you harmony with one another in Christ Jesus,
so that with one mind and one voice you may glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ.

units.label: Welcome one another as Christ welcomed you
units.note: Christ the servant of Jews and Gentiles

verses.text: Accept one another, then, just as Christ accepted you, in order to bring glory to God.
For I tell you that Christ has become a servant of the circumcised on behalf of God’s truth, to confirm the promises made to the patriarchs,
so that the Gentiles may {{doxazo|glorify}} God for His mercy. As it is written: “Therefore I will praise You among the Gentiles; I will sing hymns to Your name.”
Again, it says: “Rejoice, O Gentiles, with His people.”
And again: “Praise the Lord, all you Gentiles, and extol Him, all you peoples.”
And once more, Isaiah says: “The Root of Jesse will appear, One who will arise to rule over the Gentiles; in Him the Gentiles will put their hope.”

units.label: The God of hope fill you with joy and peace
units.note: overflowing with hope by the power of the Spirit

verses.text: Now may the God of {{elpis|hope}} fill you with all joy and peace as you believe in Him, so that you may overflow with hope by the power of the Holy Spirit.

bastazo.gloss: To carry, to bear a weight, to take up and support. It is more than tolerating; it is lifting and carrying the burden of another's weakness as one's own. The strong are called not to shrug at the failings of the weak but to shoulder them, in the pattern of the Christ who bore our burdens and did not please Himself.

oikodome.gloss: Building up, edification, the construction of a house. Paul uses this image for the growth of the church and of each believer. To please one's neighbor for his good means to act so as to build him up, laying stone upon stone in his faith, rather than tearing down the work of God for the sake of one's own preference.

doxazo.gloss: To glorify, to give glory, to honor and praise. The goal of Christ's coming to Jew and Gentile alike is that God be glorified for His mercy. The whole section is oriented to this end: that the church, welcoming one another, would with one voice glorify God, and that the nations would join Israel in praising Him.

elpis.gloss: Hope, confident expectation. Biblical hope is not wishful thinking but the settled assurance of good that God has promised. Paul calls God the God of hope and prays that believers would overflow with hope by the Spirit's power, grounding the church's unity and joy in the certain future secured by the risen Christ, the Root of Jesse in whom the nations hope.

basic.context: Paul finishes the appeal he began in chapter fourteen. Instead of the strong and the weak despising and judging one another over food and days, they are to welcome one another. He raises the bar for the strong: not merely to allow the weak but to bear their failings and seek their good. And he grounds it all in Christ, who did not please Himself but bore reproach for us, and who came as a servant so that both Jews and Gentiles would praise God together.
basic.hermeneutics: Notice how Paul argues: he moves from practical command to the example of Christ to a chain of Old Testament texts, then to a prayer. The cluster of quotations from the Law, the Psalms, and the Prophets shows that God always intended a worldwide people. Read the section as the theological summit of the whole letter's argument, where Jew and Gentile finally sing together to the glory of God.
basic.originalLanguages: The strong are to bastazo, to carry, the failings of the weak, not merely put up with them. The aim is oikodome, building up the neighbor. The great end is that all would doxazo, glorify, God for His mercy. And the closing prayer commits the church to the God of elpis, hope, who fills His people with joy and peace so that they overflow with hope by the Spirit.
basic.typology: The Root of Jesse from Isaiah is fulfilled in Christ, the Davidic King who rises to rule the nations. The promises to the patriarchs find their yes in Him. The reproaches that fell on the righteous sufferer of the Psalms fall on Christ at the cross. The Scriptures written long ago were written to give us hope, and they point beyond themselves to Him.
basic.christ: Christ is both the example and the ground. He did not please Himself but bore the insults meant for God, taking our reproach at the cross. He became a servant to the circumcised to keep God's promises and to open mercy to the Gentiles. He is the Root of Jesse in whom the nations hope. Because Christ has welcomed us at such cost, we welcome one another to the glory of God.
basic.god: God is the God who gives endurance and encouragement, the God who is glorified when His people live in harmony, and above all the God of hope. His truth and His mercy meet here: He keeps His promises to the patriarchs and He pours out mercy on the nations. The section overflows with His purpose to be praised by a united people from every tongue.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 69 supplies the reproaches that fell on Christ. Isaiah 11 promises the Root of Jesse in whom the Gentiles hope. Deuteronomy 32 and Psalms 18 and 117 call the nations to rejoice with God's people. Romans 15:13 gathers the letter's themes of faith, joy, peace, and hope.
basic.oneStory: From Abraham God promised to bless all nations, and through David He promised a King. Israel's Scriptures kept calling the nations to praise. Now in Christ, the servant of the circumcised and the Root of Jesse, the promise lands: Jew and Gentile welcome one another and glorify God together, overflowing with hope by the Spirit, a foretaste of the countless throng that will praise Him forever.

context.literary: These verses close the unit begun at 14:1, repeating the command to welcome and drawing it to a climax in verse 7, Christ welcomed you. A striking feature is the fourfold chain of Old Testament citations in verses 9 to 12, drawn from the Law, the Writings, and the Prophets, all announcing the Gentiles praising God. Two prayer-wishes frame the passage, in verses 5 to 6 and verse 13, giving it a liturgical, worshipful cadence.
context.historical: The tension between Jewish and Gentile believers in Rome lies behind the whole appeal. Paul, apostle to the Gentiles yet zealous for Israel's promises, shows that the inclusion of the nations does not cancel God's faithfulness to the patriarchs but fulfills it. He writes to a divided church to make it one worshiping body under one Lord.
context.canonical: Romans 15:8-12 is one of the New Testament's clearest windows into how the apostles read the Old Testament as pointing to a worldwide church. The citations anchor Gentile inclusion not in a break with Israel but in Israel's own Scriptures. The benediction of verse 13 has become one of the best-loved blessings in Christian worship.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call the strong to bear with and build up the weak after the pattern of the self-giving Christ, and to unite Jewish and Gentile believers in one worshiping body, showing from Scripture that God always purposed a people of every nation praising Him for His mercy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The example of Christ and the Old Testament citations describe God's saving plan; the commands to bear with the weak, please the neighbor for his good, and welcome one another are prescriptive for the church in every age, as is the pursuit of God-glorifying unity.

debates.question: In what sense did Christ not please Himself?

views.label: He bore reproach and sought our good at the cross
views.case: The historic reading takes verse 3 to mean Christ set aside His own comfort and took upon Himself the insults aimed at God, supremely at the cross, as the pattern for the strong bearing with the weak.

views.label: A general moral example of unselfishness
views.case: Some reduce it to a bare example of humility; but Paul cites Psalm 69, a psalm of the suffering righteous one, tying it specifically to Christ's redemptive suffering.

debates.bearing: The historic reading keeps the cross central: Christ's self-giving is not merely a nice example but the redemptive act that both saves us and shapes how we bear with one another.

debates.question: Do the promises to the patriarchs and the inclusion of the Gentiles stand together?

views.label: Gentile inclusion fulfills, not cancels, the promises to Israel
views.case: The historic reading holds that Christ confirms the promises made to the patriarchs and, in the same act, opens mercy to the Gentiles, exactly as the cited Scriptures foretold; God's faithfulness to Israel and His mercy to the nations are one plan.

views.label: The church simply replaces Israel
views.case: Some read Gentile inclusion as a straightforward displacement of Israel; but Paul grounds the nations' praise in Israel's own Scriptures and Israel's Messiah, the Root of Jesse.

debates.bearing: The historic reading guards God's covenant faithfulness: He keeps His word to the patriarchs and shows mercy to the nations, so Jew and Gentile glorify Him together as one people.

study.originalLanguages: Paul chooses bastazo, to carry a weight, for what the strong owe the weak: not mere tolerance but shouldering their failings as Christ shouldered ours. The goal of pleasing the neighbor is oikodome, building him up like a house rising stone by stone. The horizon of it all is that the nations would doxazo, glorify, God for His mercy, joining Israel in praise as the chain of Scripture foretold. And the whole appeal ends in the God of elpis, hope, the God who fills believing hearts with joy and peace so that, by the Spirit's power, they overflow with a hope that is sure because it rests on the risen Root of Jesse.

typology.type: The suffering righteous one of Psalm 69
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, on whom the reproaches meant for God fell, supremely at the cross, Psalm 69:9.
Fulfilled in Christ, the Davidic King who rises to rule the nations and in whom the Gentiles hope, Isaiah 11:10.
Confirmed in Christ, who became a servant of the circumcised so that God's ancient promises find their yes and reach the nations.

study.christ: Christ is the beating heart of the passage. He did not please Himself but took upon Himself the reproaches aimed at God, the language of the suffering psalmist fulfilled at the cross. He became a servant of the circumcised to confirm the promises to the patriarchs, and in the same saving work opened the door of mercy to the Gentiles. He is the Root of Jesse, the risen King in whom the nations put their hope. His welcome of sinners at infinite cost is the pattern and the power for our welcome of one another, all to the glory of God.
study.god: God is glorified as the God of truth and the God of mercy: faithful to His promises to Israel and merciful to the nations, He gathers one people to praise Him with one voice. He is the God who gives endurance and encouragement through the Scriptures, and He is the God of hope who fills His people with joy and peace. The section is thoroughly God-centered, aiming at His glory and resting in His character as the sure ground of the church's unity and hope.

crossReferences.note: The reproaches that fell on the righteous sufferer, applied by Paul to Christ who did not please Himself.
The Root of Jesse who will stand as a signal for the peoples and in whom the nations hope, fulfilled in Christ.
Rejoice, O nations, with His people, one of the Scriptures Paul chains together to show the Gentiles praising God.
The promise that all nations would be blessed through Abraham, whose fulfillment Paul sees in the Gentiles glorifying God.

study.oneStory: God promised Abraham that all nations would be blessed, and David that a King would come. Israel's Scriptures repeatedly summoned the nations to praise. In Christ, the servant of the circumcised and the Root of Jesse, that promise is kept: Jew and Gentile welcome one another and glorify God together for His mercy. Overflowing with hope by the Spirit, the church becomes a living preview of the countless people from every nation who will one day sing to God with one voice.

quiz.q: What are the strong called to do for the weak in verses 1 and 2?

options.text: Bear with their shortcomings and please them for their good, to build them up.
options.note: The strong are to carry the weak and seek their edification, not their own pleasure.

options.text: Tolerate them while quietly pleasing themselves.
options.note: Bearing with the weak is right, but Paul says not to please ourselves; the aim is their good and their upbuilding.

options.text: Leave them alone to work out their own faith.
options.note: Paul calls for active bearing and building up, not detachment.

options.text: Insist they abandon their scruples immediately.
options.note: The command is to bear with them, not to demand they change at once.

quiz.q: What example does Paul give to ground this call?

options.text: Christ did not please Himself but bore the reproaches meant for God.
options.note: Paul cites Psalm 69 to point to Christ's self-giving as the pattern.

options.text: The example of the Old Testament kings.
options.note: Paul points to Christ, the Root of Jesse, not to the kings as models here.

options.text: His own personal sacrifices as an apostle.
options.note: Paul does model self-giving, but the example he grounds this in is Christ, who did not please Himself.

options.text: The Roman believers' past record of unity.
options.note: The example is Christ who did not please Himself.

quiz.q: According to verses 8 and 9, why did Christ become a servant of the circumcised?

options.text: To confirm the promises to the patriarchs and so that the Gentiles might glorify God for His mercy.
options.note: Christ keeps the promises to Israel and opens mercy to the nations.

options.text: To replace Israel entirely with the Gentile church.
options.note: Paul grounds Gentile praise in Israel's Scriptures, not in displacing Israel.

options.text: To abolish God's promises to the patriarchs.
options.note: Christ came to confirm those promises, not abolish them.

options.text: To serve only the Jewish people and no others.
options.note: Christ did serve the circumcised to confirm the promises, but the goal reaches further, that the Gentiles too might glorify God for His mercy.

quiz.q: Why does Paul chain together four Old Testament quotations in verses 9 to 12?

options.text: To show from Scripture that God always purposed Jew and Gentile to praise Him together.
options.note: The Law, Psalms, and Prophets all foretell the nations praising God.

options.text: To prove that the Old Testament is no longer relevant.
options.note: Paul treats the Scriptures as authoritative and fulfilled, not obsolete.

options.text: To settle a dispute about which foods are clean.
options.note: The wider context concerns the weak and the strong over food, but here the quotations aim to show Jew and Gentile praising God together.

options.text: To display his skill at quoting texts.
options.note: The purpose is theological, to ground Gentile inclusion in Scripture.

quiz.q: What does Paul pray for in the benediction of verse 13?

options.text: That the God of hope would fill them with joy and peace so they overflow with hope by the Spirit.
options.note: The blessing gathers joy, peace, and overflowing hope by the Spirit's power.

options.text: That they would gain worldly success and comfort.
options.note: The prayer is for joy, peace, and hope in God, not worldly gain.

options.text: That they would simply feel more optimistic.
options.note: Paul prays for real joy, peace, and overflowing hope by the Spirit, which is far more than mere optimism.

options.text: That they would be freed from all future suffering.
options.note: The hope is by the Spirit amid life, not a promise of no suffering now.

questions: Paul calls the strong not merely to tolerate the weak but to bear their failings, as Christ bore ours. Whose burden is God asking you to shoulder rather than shrug off?
Christ did not please Himself but took the reproaches meant for God. Where is God calling you to lay down your own comfort or preference for the good of another believer?
We are to welcome one another just as Christ welcomed us. How does the cost and warmth of Christ's welcome of you change the way you receive people you find difficult?
Paul shows from Scripture that God always planned to gather every nation to praise Him. How does belonging to that worldwide, promised people shape your view of your local church?

The God of hope wants to fill you with joy and peace so that you overflow with hope by the Spirit. Where do you most need that overflowing hope right now, and how might you seek it in believing prayer?

translationNotes.note: The quotation is from Psalm 69:9, a psalm of the suffering righteous one; Paul applies the reproaches that fell on the psalmist to Christ, who bore the insults meant for God.
Paul strings together Psalm 18:49 with 2 Samuel 22:50, then Deuteronomy 32:43 and Psalm 117:1, drawing from the Law, the Writings, and the Prophets to show the nations praising God with Israel.
The Root of Jesse is from Isaiah 11:10; the wording follows the Greek Old Testament, which speaks of the nations hoping in Him, fulfilled in the risen Christ, the Davidic King over the Gentiles.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 15:1-13. Poetic quotation lines in verses 9 to 12 were concatenated into the running verse text with single spaces. No em dashes required substitution. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
